response: Enacted in May 2018, the EU’s GDPR fundamentally reshaped data privacy by requiring strict user consent, 72-hour breach reporting, and expansive data rights for any organization processing EU residents' information. Violations carry penalties of up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million, resulting in landmark fines against tech giants like Meta (€1.2 billion), Amazon (€746 million), and Google (over €200 million). While critics argue the regulation imposes disproportionate compliance costs on small businesses, suffers from inconsistent enforcement, and frustrates users with cookie banners, it has successfully established data privacy as a fundamental right. Ultimately, GDPR set the global benchmark for digital protection, directly inspiring similar legislation in California, Brazil, and India.
